summary: Justin Brennan provides an in-depth walkthrough of analyzing a 33-unit Class C apartment complex (The Oasis) in Phoenix, Arizona listed at $7 million. The session covers how to read operating memorandums, stress-test financial projections, understand debt coverage ratios, and use proformas to determine if a deal makes sense regardless of asking price. Key insight: commercial multifamily financing is based on property financials (not borrower financials), which changes deal analysis fundamentally.
takeaways: Five critical steps to buying apartments: finding deals, analyzing properly, raising capital (debt + equity), closing escrow, and asset management
5+ units threshold: banks underwrite on property financials, not borrower financials; non-recourse loans where property (not you) pays mortgage
Properties valued as: NOI ÷ cap rate = property value; buy at higher cap rate, renovate/increase rents to lower cap rate, sell at lower cap rate for profit spread
Stress test using debt service coverage ratio (DCR) minimum 1.25x: NOI must be 125% of annual debt payments or bank won't finance
Expense ratio: target 30-31% of effective gross income (EGI) for Class C properties; verify property taxes and insurance with local brokers/assessors
Interest-only loan periods (2-3 years minimum): prioritizes investor cash flow over principal paydown since property sold in 3-5 year hold
Cost segregation study ($5-8k): accelerates building depreciation over 3-5 years instead of 27.5 years, creating paper losses that offset capital gains/ordinary income for tax professionals
Only buy in top 75 MSAs (Metropolitan Statistical Areas) with strong job/population growth; avoid tertiary markets that collapse during recessions
Current market metrics for Phoenix: 5-6% cap rate range, 5.75-6.5% interest rates, $100-200k per unit acquisition prices, target 2%+ cap rate spread between acquisition and disposition

skill_candidates.change: Use 5% vacancy loss + 1-2% other losses (concessions/bad debt) = 7% total economic loss assumption when underwriting
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states 'I always will underwrite to a five and the reason why is because the bank's going to underwrite to a 5' and shows how to calculate total economic loss percentage in proforma

skill_candidates.change: Verify DCR ≥1.25x before proceeding with deal; if DCR drops below 1.25 with interest rate changes, reduce purchase price until DCR meets minimum
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin demonstrates live: 'so the bank is going to want to make sure that number is 1.25 times more than your debt service' and shows adjusting purchase price from $7M to $6.2M when interest rates rise to maintain 1.25 DCR

skill_candidates.change: Use sensitivity analysis on interest rates (test 5%, 5.75%, 6%, 6.5%) to establish maximum offer price rather than anchoring to seller's ask
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin shows changing interest rate from 5.75% to 6% drops max purchase price from $6.7M to $6.2M, demonstrating 'it's not about opinion...the numbers either make sense or they don't'

skill_candidates.change: Maintain 2% minimum spread between acquisition cap rate and projected disposition cap rate (buy at 6%, sell at 4-5%) to ensure profit capture
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states 'you want it to be as low as possible when selling' and shows buying at 6% cap rate and selling at 4% generates significantly higher IRR than buying at 6% and selling at 6%

skill_candidates.change: Confirm market cap rates, property tax assessment impact on expenses, and insurance costs with local commercial brokers/assessors before underwriting
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin emphasizes: 'property taxes same thing big big big numbers...you want to have an insurance broker that you can run it before you would actually close' and shows property tax/insurance changed due to county reassessment on sale

title: Unlocking The Secrets Of Multifamily Investing: A Real-life 33-unit Case Study With Justin Brennan
